covered_sections: Scalars and Vectors>1.1 Scalars and Vectors
Vector addition and subtraction>1.3 Vector addition and subtraction
Graphical method of vector addition>1.4 Graphical method of vector addition

distribution_analysis.most_covered_section: Vector addition and subtraction, Vector resolution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a vector quantity?
questions.hint: Look for a quantity that includes a specified direction.
questions.explanation: Displacement includes both magnitude (5 m) and direction (north), making it a vector. Mass, time, and temperature are scalars as they have magnitude only.

explanations_tiered.definition: A vector quantity is a physical quantity that has both magnitude and direction and follows vector addition rules.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Vectors require directional information. Displacement, velocity, and force are common vector examples.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a weather report: '30 km/h wind' is scalar speed; '30 km/h north' is vector velocity.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Displacement (5 m north) specifies both how far and which way. Mass (10 kg) and temperature (20°C) have no direction, so they are scalars.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Vector quantities transform under coordinate rotations according to \(V'_i = R_{ij}V_j\), unlike scalars which are invariant.
reasoning_steps: Identify if direction is stated.
If direction is given, the quantity is a vector.
Select the option with both magnitude and direction.
misconceptions: Some think speed (scalar) and velocity (vector) are the same. Speed lacks direction.
Distance (scalar) vs displacement (vector) is another common confusion.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Vectors have magnitude AND direction. Scalars have magnitude only.

glossary_entry.definition: A physical quantity such as displacement, velocity, acceleration, or force that requires both magnitude and direction.

flashcard.front: Give an example of a vector quantity.
flashcard.back: Displacement (e.g., 5 m north), velocity (20 m/s east), or force (10 N downward).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Give an example of a vector quantity.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Displacement (5 m north), velocity, force, acceleration.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A quantity with both magnitude and direction, such as displacement or velocity.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Vector = magnitude + direction. Example: 5 m north.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Have students classify a list of quantities as scalar or vector.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Differentiate between scalar and vector quantities and give examples
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Displacement includes direction (north), so it is a vector.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What does the length of an arrow represent when drawing a vector geometrically?
questions.hint: In a scale drawing, the arrow's length corresponds to the numerical size.
questions.explanation: In geometric representation, the arrow length, drawn to scale, represents the vector's magnitude. The arrow head indicates direction. Option B (direction) is shown by arrow orientation, not length.

source_trace.content_anchor: length of the arrow represents the vector magnitude

explanations_tiered.definition: In graphical vector representation, the length of the arrow, drawn to an appropriate scale, represents the magnitude of the vector.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Vectors are drawn as arrows: length shows how large the quantity is; arrow head shows which way it points.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If 1 cm on paper = 10 km in real life, a 3 cm arrow means 30 km magnitude.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Geometric vector representation uses scale: \(\text{drawn length} = \frac{\text{actual magnitude}}{\text{scale}}\). The arrow length is proportional to magnitude.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The magnitude \(|\vec{v}|\) is mapped to a length \(L\) via a scale factor \(k\): \(L = k|\vec{v}|\). The direction is given by the unit vector \(\hat{v}\).
reasoning_steps: Identify that vectors are drawn as arrows.
Recall that length corresponds to how large the quantity is.
Select 'magnitude' as the correct property.
misconceptions: Some students think arrow length represents direction. Direction is shown by arrow orientation, not length.
A longer arrow means larger magnitude, not necessarily more important.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Arrow length = magnitude. Arrow direction = vector direction.

glossary_entry.definition: The length of the arrow in a vector diagram, drawn to scale, representing the size of the vector quantity.

flashcard.front: What does the length of a vector arrow represent?
flashcard.back: The vector's magnitude (size). Direction is shown by where the arrow points.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does arrow length show in a vector diagram?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The vector's magnitude (size). Example: longer arrow = larger magnitude.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A geometric drawing where vectors are represented by arrows whose length indicates magnitude and orientation indicates direction.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Arrow length = magnitude; arrow direction = vector direction.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw several arrows of different lengths pointing same direction to illustrate.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Arrow length shows magnitude; arrow direction shows direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two vectors have equal magnitude. What is the maximum possible magnitude of their sum?

options.B: Equal to the magnitude of one vector
options.C: Twice the magnitude of one vector
options.D: Half the magnitude of one vector

questions.hint: Consider when two vectors point in exactly the same direction.
questions.explanation: When vectors are parallel and in the same direction, the resultant magnitude is the sum: \(R = A + B = A + A = 2A\). The maximum sum occurs at \(0^\circ\) angle.

source_trace.content_anchor: If the two vectors are in the same direction, the magnitude of their sum will be a maximum

explanations_tiered.definition: The maximum resultant magnitude of two vectors occurs when they are parallel and in the same direction, giving \(R_{\text{max}} = A + B\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For equal magnitudes \(A = B\), \(R_{\text{max}} = A + A = 2A\). The minimum is \(0\) when opposite.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Two people pushing a box the same way: forces add. If each pushes with 10 N, total = 20 N = twice one push.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From law of cosines: \(R^2 = A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta\). Maximum when \(\cos\theta = 1\) (\(\theta = 0^\circ\)): \(R = A + B = 2A\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The sum magnitude \(|\vec{A} + \vec{B}| \le |\vec{A}| + |\vec{B}|\) by triangle inequality. Equality holds iff vectors are parallel and same direction.
reasoning_steps: Recall resultant depends on angle between vectors.
Maximum when angle = 0° (same direction).
Add magnitudes: \(R = A + B = 2A\).
misconceptions: Some think maximum sum is just one vector's magnitude (forgetting addition).
Others think maximum is less than sum due to direction, but same direction gives full sum.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Max sum: same direction, \(R = A + B\). For equal \(A\), \(R_{\text{max}} = 2A\).

glossary_entry.definition: The greatest possible magnitude of the sum of two vectors, achieved when they are parallel and in the same direction.

flashcard.front: Two equal-magnitude vectors: when is their sum maximum?
flashcard.back: When they point in the same direction (0° apart). Sum = 2 × magnitude of one.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: When is the sum of two equal vectors maximum?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: When they are parallel and same direction: \(R_{\text{max}} = 2A\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The largest possible resultant magnitude, achieved at 0° angle between vectors.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Max sum: same direction, \(R = A + B = 2A\) if equal.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use two 5 N forces: same direction = 10 N; opposite = 0 N; perpendicular ≈ 7.07 N.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Add two vectors and understand resultant magnitude extremes
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Maximum when same direction: \(R = A + B = 2A\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If \(\vec{A} = 10\text{ N}\) east and \(\vec{B} = 4\text{ N}\) east, what is \(\vec{A} - \vec{B}\)?
questions.hint: Subtracting a vector means adding its negative.
questions.explanation: \(\vec{A} - \vec{B} = \vec{A} + (-\vec{B})\). \(-\vec{B}\) is \(4\text{ N}\) west. Adding \(10\text{ N}\) east and \(4\text{ N}\) west gives \(6\text{ N}\) east. Option A is \(\vec{A} + \vec{B}\).

explanations_tiered.definition: Vector subtraction is defined as adding the negative of the subtrahend: \(\vec{A} - \vec{B} = \vec{A} + (-\vec{B})\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The negative of a vector has same magnitude but opposite direction. Subtraction reverses the direction of the second vector before adding.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you walk 10 m east then go back 4 m east, you end up 6 m east. Subtracting 4 m east means going opposite direction 4 m west.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\vec{A} - \vec{B} = \vec{A} + (-\vec{B})\). With east positive, \(\vec{A} = +10\), \(\vec{B} = +4\), \(-\vec{B} = -4\). Sum = \(+10 + (-4) = +6\) N east.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In component form: \((A_x - B_x)\hat{i} + (A_y - B_y)\hat{j}\). Here \(A_x=10, B_x=4\), so \(A_x-B_x=6\), result east.
worked_solution: Step 1: Set east as positive direction.
Step 4: Positive means east, magnitude 6 N.
reasoning_steps: Determine direction convention.
Add negative of second vector.
Combine magnitudes using signs.
Determine final direction from sign.
misconceptions: Students often subtract magnitudes without considering direction: 10 - 4 = 6, but direction wrong if vectors not parallel.
Some think \(\vec{A} - \vec{B}\) always same direction as \(\vec{A}\) (not true if \(\vec{B}\) larger opposite).

glossary_entry.definition: The operation of adding the negative of one vector to another: \(\vec{A} - \vec{B} = \vec{A} + (-\vec{B})\).

flashcard.front: How do you subtract vector \(\vec{B}\) from \(\vec{A}\)?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Adding the negative of a vector to another: \(\vec{A} - \vec{B} = \vec{A} + (-\vec{B})\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In the triangle law of vector addition, the resultant vector is represented by which side of the triangle?

options.C: The third side taken in the opposite order

questions.hint: The resultant closes the triangle from start to finish.
questions.explanation: The triangle law states: if two vectors are represented by two sides of a triangle in order, the third side taken in the opposite order gives the resultant. Option C matches this definition.

source_trace.content_anchor: resultant sum vector is given by the third side of that triangle taken in the opposite order

explanations_tiered.definition: The triangle law of vector addition states that if two vectors are represented by two sides of a triangle taken in order, the third side taken in the opposite order represents their resultant.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The resultant closes the triangle, going from the tail of the first to the head of the second.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Draw first arrow, then second arrow from first's tip. The arrow from start to final tip is the resultant — the triangle's third side.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: If \(\vec{A}\) and \(\vec{B}\) form two sides of a triangle in order (head-to-tail), the closing side from tail of \(\vec{A}\) to head of \(\vec{B}\) is \(\vec{R} = \vec{A} + \vec{B}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Triangle law is a special case of the polygon law for two vectors. Graphically, \(\vec{R} = \vec{A} + \vec{B}\) satisfies closure: \(\vec{A} + \vec{B} - \vec{R} = 0\).
reasoning_steps: Recall triangle law: two sides in order, third side opposite order = resultant.
Identify which side closes the triangle from start to finish.
Select 'third side opposite order'.
misconceptions: Some think resultant is the sum of all sides (perimeter).
Direction of resultant matters: opposite order is critical.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Triangle law: third side opposite order = resultant.

glossary_entry.definition: If two vectors are represented by two sides of a triangle taken in order, the third side taken in the opposite order represents their resultant.

flashcard.front: In triangle law, which side is the resultant?
flashcard.back: The third side taken in opposite order (closing side).

visual_system.visual_description: Triangle with sides A (horizontal), B (at angle from tip of A), and R (closing side from tail of A to head of B).
visual_system.caption: Figure: Triangle law - third side opposite order gives resultant.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Triangle diagram. Side A from left to right. Side B from tip of A upward right. Side R from start of A to tip of B (closing side).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: In triangle law, what represents the resultant?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The third side of the triangle taken in opposite order.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A rule for adding two vectors graphically using a triangle where the closing side is the resultant.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Triangle law: third side opposite order = resultant \(\vec{R}\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw triangle on board; emphasize 'closing side' from start to end.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the graphical method of vector addition
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Resultant is third side opposite order (closing side).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A boy walks \(3\text{ km}\) east then \(2\text{ km}\) north. What is the magnitude of his displacement?
questions.hint: Use the Pythagorean theorem for perpendicular displacements.
questions.explanation: Displacement is the vector sum of perpendicular components. \(R = \sqrt{(3)^2 + (2)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 4} = \sqrt{13} = 3.61\text{ km}\). Option B (5 km) is the scalar sum, not vector sum.

calculation_steps: East displacement = 3 km, north displacement = 2 km (perpendicular).

explanations_tiered.definition: The magnitude of the resultant of two perpendicular vectors is found using the Pythagorean theorem: \(R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When displacements are perpendicular, the direct displacement (hypotenuse) is less than the sum of distances.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Walk 3 km east, then 2 km north. You're not 5 km from start — more like 3.6 km straight-line distance.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: East and north are perpendicular, so displacement magnitude = \(\sqrt{3^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{13} = 3.61\) km. Direction: \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(2/3) = 33.7^\circ\) north of east.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Position vector \(\vec{r} = (3, 2)\) km. Magnitude \(|\vec{r}| = \sqrt{3^2 + 2^2}\). This is the Euclidean norm in 2D.
worked_solution: Step 1: Draw east vector = 3 km, north vector = 2 km (90° apart).
Step 2: Displacement = hypotenuse of right triangle.
reasoning_steps: Identify that east and north are perpendicular.
Apply Pythagorean theorem.
Calculate squares: 9 + 4 = 13.
Take square root to find magnitude.
misconceptions: Adding distances (3+2=5 km) gives total path length, not displacement.
Forgetting that displacement is straight-line from start to end.

glossary_entry.definition: The straight-line distance from initial to final position, found via Pythagorean theorem for perpendicular components.

flashcard.front: 3 km east then 2 km north: displacement magnitude?

visual_system.visual_description: Right triangle: horizontal leg 3 km east, vertical leg 2 km north, hypotenuse 3.61 km.
visual_system.caption: Figure: Displacement magnitude from perpendicular components.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Right triangle: east 3 km (horizontal), north 2 km (vertical), hypotenuse 3.61 km representing straight-line displacement.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 3 km east + 2 km north = ? displacement magnitude
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The straight-line vector from start to finish, magnitude found by Pythagorean theorem for perpendicular legs.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast distance (5 km) vs displacement (3.61 km).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Find the resultant of two or more vectors using component method

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In the parallelogram method, where are the tails of the two vectors placed?

options.C: At opposite ends of the diagonal
options.D: At the vertices of the parallelogram

questions.hint: Both vectors start from the same point in this method.
questions.explanation: In the parallelogram method, two vectors are drawn from the same origin (common point) to form adjacent sides of a parallelogram. Option B describes triangle/head-to-tail method.

source_trace.content_anchor: vectors that are to be added form the two adjacent sides of a parallelogram by joining the tails of the two vectors

explanations_tiered.definition: The parallelogram method places the tails of two vectors at a common origin, making them adjacent sides of a parallelogram; the diagonal from the origin is the resultant.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Unlike head-to-tail, parallelogram method starts both vectors from same point, then completes the parallelogram.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Draw two arrows from the same dot. Complete the parallelogram. The diagonal from the dot to opposite corner is the combined vector.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For vectors \(\vec{A}\) and \(\vec{B}\) from same point, complete parallelogram. Diagonal \(\vec{R} = \vec{A} + \vec{B}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Parallelogram law: \(\vec{R} = \vec{A} + \vec{B}\). The diagonal represents the sum; the other diagonal is \(\vec{A} - \vec{B}\).
reasoning_steps: Recall parallelogram method places tails together.
Complete parallelogram by drawing parallel lines.
Resultant is diagonal from common origin.
misconceptions: Some confuse parallelogram method with head-to-tail (triangle method).
Thinking the other diagonal (not from origin) is the resultant.

glossary_entry.definition: A graphical method where two vectors from a common point form adjacent sides of a parallelogram; the diagonal from that point is their sum.

flashcard.front: Where do tails go in parallelogram method?
flashcard.back: Both tails at the same point (common origin).

visual_system.visual_description: Two vectors from same point, forming sides of parallelogram. Diagonal from origin is resultant.
visual_system.caption: Figure: Parallelogram law of vector addition.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Parallelogram: vectors A and B from same origin. Diagonal R from origin to opposite corner is resultant.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How are vectors placed in parallelogram method?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Both tails at same point (common origin).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The rule that the sum of two vectors from a common point is the diagonal of the parallelogram formed.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show both triangle and parallelogram methods to contrast.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the graphical method of vector addition
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Parallelogram method places both tails at same point.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which formula correctly gives the magnitude of a vector from its components \(A_x\) and \(A_y\)?
questions.hint: This is the Pythagorean theorem applied to vector components.
questions.explanation: The magnitude of a vector from its perpendicular components is given by \(A = \sqrt{A_x^2 + A_y^2}\). Option A and C give the scalar sum, not the vector magnitude.

explanations_tiered.definition: The magnitude of a vector is the square root of the sum of the squares of its perpendicular components.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This is a direct application of the Pythagorean theorem to the right triangle formed by components.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a vector has an x-part of 3 and y-part of 4, its total length is \(\sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = 5\), not 3+4=7.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The magnitude is the Euclidean norm: \(\|\vec{A}\| = \sqrt{\sum_i A_i^2}\) in 2D.
reasoning_steps: Recall components are perpendicular.
Apply Pythagorean theorem.
Take square root of sum of squares.

formula_analysis.name: Magnitude from components
formula_analysis.derivation: From Pythagorean theorem on right triangle

misconceptions: Adding components directly gives incorrect magnitude.
Forgetting to take square root (using \(A_x^2 + A_y^2\) without sqrt).

glossary_entry.definition: The length of a vector computed as the square root of the sum of squares of its components.

flashcard.front: How do you find magnitude from components \(A_x, A_y\)?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The magnitude of a vector equals the square root of the sum of squares of its components.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A person walks 3 km east then 2 km north. What is the direction of the displacement relative to east?
questions.hint: Angle from east = opposite/adjacent with respect to east axis.
questions.explanation: Displacement components: east = 3 km (adjacent), north = 2 km (opposite). \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(\frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}) = \tan^{-1}(2/3)\) north of east. Option C swaps axis reference.

calculation_steps: East component = 3 km (adjacent), north component = 2 km (opposite).

explanations_tiered.definition: The direction of a vector from its components is given by \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(A_y/A_x)\) measured from the positive x-axis.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The tangent of the angle equals opposite (y-component) over adjacent (x-component).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Walk 3 km east, 2 km north. You end up northeast. The exact angle from east is found by \(\tan^{-1}(2/3)\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: With east as x-axis, north as y-axis: \(\tan\theta = \frac{\text{north}}{\text{east}} = \frac{2}{3}\), so \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(2/3) = 33.7^\circ\) north of east.
reasoning_steps: Determine which axis is reference (east).
Opposite = north component, adjacent = east component.
Apply \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(\text{opposite}/\text{adjacent})\).
Express as 'north of east'.

formula_analysis.name: Direction from components
formula_analysis.derivation: From definition of tangent in right triangle

misconceptions: Using \(\tan^{-1}(A_x/A_y)\) instead of \(A_y/A_x\).
Mislabeling angle (east of north vs north of east).

glossary_entry.definition: The angle a vector makes with the positive x-axis, computed as \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(A_y/A_x)\).

flashcard.front: 3 km east, 2 km north: direction from east?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 3 km east, 2 km north: angle from east?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The angle a vector makes with the positive x-axis, found using the inverse tangent of y/x.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw right triangle: east horizontal, north vertical.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. \(\tan\theta = 2/3\), so \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(2/3)\) north of east.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why can a vector be resolved into components along any chosen set of perpendicular axes?

options.B: Because the components are independent and their sum equals the original vector
options.C: Because components always have the same magnitude

questions.hint: Components are projections that add to give the original vector.
questions.explanation: Vector resolution relies on the fact that perpendicular components are independent and their vector sum equals the original. Option B correctly states this principle.

source_trace.content_anchor: Any vector can be resolved into a horizontal and a vertical component

explanations_tiered.definition: Vector resolution is the process of breaking a vector into perpendicular components whose vector sum equals the original vector.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Components are independent but together reconstruct the original exactly.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you walk 5 m northeast, you can think of it as walking some distance east plus some distance north. Together they give the same final position.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\vec{A} = \vec{A}_x + \vec{A}_y\) where \(A_x = A\cos\theta\), \(A_y = A\sin\theta\). Components sum to original.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In linear algebra, any vector in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) can be expressed as a linear combination of basis vectors: \(\vec{A} = A_x\hat{i} + A_y\hat{j}\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that perpendicular components are projections.
Understand that adding components recovers original.
Select reason: components sum to original vector.
misconceptions: Thinking components are physically separate vectors, not mathematical projections.
Believing resolution changes the vector (it doesn't — it decomposes it).

glossary_entry.definition: The process of expressing a vector as the sum of its perpendicular components along chosen axes.

flashcard.front: Why can any vector be resolved into components?
flashcard.back: Because perpendicular components are independent and their vector sum equals the original vector.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why can vectors be resolved into components?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Perpendicular components are independent and sum to original.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Decomposing a vector into perpendicular components that sum to the original.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain that components are not real — they are mathematical tools.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Resolve a vector into its components
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Components are independent and their vector sum equals the original vector.
